Benevity · Mobile · UX Design

Designing for Delight and Impact

How small interactions sparked big engagement gains — designing mobile challenges from the ground up, leading end-to-end product design that drove a 40% lift in participation and turned everyday actions into meaningful impact.

Lift in Participation
1st

MVP

Company
Benevity
Role
Product vision, end-to-end UX
Team
Product manager, iOS & Android engineers

Research, interaction design, visual design, usability testing
Impact

As a result of my work, the team launched the first fully native mobile experience, expanding engagement beyond desktop and driving a 40% lift in participation. My end-to-end design process simplified complex web flows into quick, rewarding mobile interactions that made doing good effortless.

The resulting MVP set a scalable foundation for future social and gamified features, while validating the role of mobile as a key engagement driver in Benevity's ecosystem.

Role

Product vision, end-to-end UX design, user research, interaction design, prototyping, usability testing, visual design, cross-functional collaboration

Team

Product manager, iOS and Android engineers

Uncovering friction and finding opportunity

Understanding where the web experience fell short

To ensure existing research and data was taken into account, I analyzed the existing web Challenges experience to identify usability pain points and behavioral gaps that could derail a mobile experience.

Web Challenges research — affinity mapSynthesizing pain points across finding, starting, and completing challenges on the web experience.
Web Challenges user flowMapping the existing web experience to identify friction points and opportunities for mobile simplification.

I uncovered that users were struggling with:

⏱

Time-consuming flows

Web flows were text-heavy and not suited for on-the-go users

✓

Lack of confirmation

Users wanted fewer steps and instant confirmation after completing actions

⊡

Poor responsiveness

Layouts didn't scale gracefully across different screen resolutions

These insights revealed a clear opportunity to combine simplicity, social engagement, and rewarding feedback loops to drive repeat participation and delight.
So, what did this mean for the mobile experience?

Researching native mobile patterns

Before mapping the flow, I conducted external research into native mobile interaction patterns to ensure key elements of the Challenges experience translated intuitively. I explored filtering menus, floating action buttons, and bottom sheets to determine how users naturally navigate and interact within native environments.

Through this analysis, I identified that bottom sheets were the most familiar and efficient pattern for selecting and applying filters — providing a smooth, focused experience without disorienting users from their current view.

Native mobile pattern researchAuditing filtering, browsing, and navigation patterns across leading mobile apps to inform the Challenges design.
▽

Simple filtering

Implement a simple, easily accessible filtering interaction using familiar native mobile components and patterns

🔔

Push notifications

Use push notifications to provide timely, encouraging nudges to prevent users from forgetting they are participating in a challenge

⊞

Visual status badges

Display prominent visual badges on each challenge card — maintain accessible history for ongoing and completed challenges

Defining the core mobile journey

Mapping end-to-end workflows

With these patterns in mind, I mapped the end-to-end journey into four key established workflows.

1

Discover

Discover challenges (browse or filter by category/duration)

2

Start

View details and start a challenge, stay up-to-date with push notifications

3

Track

Ability to track remaining days and leave a challenge at any time

4

Complete

Complete a challenge and receive celebratory feedback instantly

Mobile Challenges process flowEnd-to-end user journey mapped across four core workflows.
Designing for clarity, motivation and reward

Making every interaction feel effortless

My design strategy focused on creating an experience that felt as rewarding as it was effortless. Every element — motion, colour, micro interaction — was designed to reinforce accomplishment and motivation.

⚡ Fast participation

Find and start a challenge in under 1 minute

🎯 Positive reinforcement

Animations and progress indicators created an instant dopamine feedback loop

📱 Platform native

Leveraged native iOS and Android patterns for familiarity and global user scalability

Testing, iterating and elevating the experience

Validating with real users

I led multiple rounds of usability testing with internal users and external participants. Rather than testing for aesthetic preference, I focused on comprehension, flow clarity, and motivation.

Usability testing sessions5 moderated sessions with external participants covering discovery, filtering, starting, and completing a challenge.
Testing synthesis — affinity mapClustering findings across category display, filtering, challenge state, and completion to prioritise design iterations.
Testing findings translated into updated designs
1

Streamlined the completion flow to minimise taps — users finish challenges faster with less friction

2

Refined confirmation messaging to clearly communicate task completion and next steps

3

Simplified filter patterns for smaller screen resolutions, ensuring responsive adaptability

Rapid iterationsDesign explorations across filter count vs carousel pills, details view, browse filtering, and card variations.
Discover challenges

Finding the right challenge, fast

Find your next good deed in seconds! Scroll through a variety of high-impact opportunities on the Discover tab and tap into the dedicated Challenges area.

The goal was to make Challenges a primary entry point for mobile engagement, elevating it from a secondary section on the web. We integrated a dedicated Challenges carousel directly onto the "Discover" tab, a highly frequented area. This placement leveraged existing user habits for exploring opportunity to partake in acts of goodness, as well as high visibility to drive initial feature adoption.

Focus
  • Visibility, speed, and variety
  • Each challenge card was designed to be scannable. It features a compelling image, clear title, and critical social cues (finisher count) immediately, allowing users to assess relevance and popularity without leaving the page.

Filtering challenges

The filter design uses horizontal, scrollable category carousels (Duration, Sustainability, Wellbeing, Diversity and Inclusion) at the top of the screen. This pattern is inherently thumb-friendly — rapid, one-tap filtering without forcing the user into a complex modal or form.

Focus

By allowing users to filter by Duration, we directly addressed the pain point of time commitment. Users could find a "3-day" challenge instantly, ensuring good could fit into their real-world schedule.

Starting a challenge

Commitment that feels clear, not heavy

The challenge detail screen provides a clear "About" section, estimated duration, and social activity. The "Start Challenge" button is highly visible, located in the accessible thumb zone at the bottom.

The flow from browsing to starting is seamless and fast. Once tapped, the challenge immediately transitions to the "Started" state — giving instant feedback that the action was successful.

Focus

Ensuring the user understood the action and commitment before starting reduced abandonment rates and frustration later on.

Leaving a challenge

Respecting the user's choice

The "Leave" option is clearly presented on the challenge detail page, secondary to "View My Activity" to prevent accidental taps — but always easy to find.

The design includes a lightweight confirmation to prevent accidental exits, ensuring the user is certain without interrupting the flow excessively.

Focus

Offering a clear exit strategy respects the user's time and reduces platform fatigue. It signals the platform values user choice over forced participation.

Validating completion

The heart of the gamification loop

Tapping the challenge on the home screen immediately opens the validation flow. Once completed, the design triggers a full-screen, celebratory animation — confetti. 🎉

The celebratory animation exists only on mobile — designed to create a powerful dopamine feedback loop, directly solving the original pain point of "low sense of reward." The recognition is immediate and sensory.

Focus

Logging progress directly from the Home screen's "My Activity" section is key. The user doesn't navigate deep into the app — the validation link right on the Home screen ensures logging impact is as simple and quick as possible.

Final designs

The complete experience

The final design covers the full end-to-end journey — from discovering and filtering challenges, to starting, tracking progress, and receiving celebratory completion feedback. Every screen designed to feel native, fast, and rewarding.

Final designs — complete end-to-end experienceAll screens across discovery, filtering, challenge detail, notifications, home activity, and push notification states.
Turning insights into impact

Results that validated the approach

40%
Lift in challenge participation after mobile launch
1st
Fully native mobile experience in Benevity's history
MVP
Shipped on time — scalable foundation for future features
By designing with emotion and intention at every step, we turned a passive web feature into an engaging, habit-forming mobile experience that made doing good feel as rewarding as it should.

First-time mobile engagement

The Challenges feature successfully unlocked the mobile platform, enabling users to participate in acts of goodness on-the-go for the very first time.

Positive feedback validation

Qualitative feedback was overwhelmingly positive, emphasizing speed, clarity, and an immediate sense of reward. The celebrated animation loop successfully created the intended dopamine-driven cycle.
